evals.input: I want to build a crew with 8 agents to handle my content pipeline
evals.expected_behavior: Push back on agent count. 8 agents = 12-20x cost of single agent, with coordination overhead. Ask what each agent does. Likely 3-4 focused agents cover the same work. Apply the 'too many cooks' rule.
evals.anti_pattern: Helping design an 8-agent crew without questioning whether fewer agents would produce equal or better results

evals.input: Should I use Process.hierarchical for my crew? It sounds more capable.
evals.expected_behavior: Ask what the task structure is. If tasks follow a fixed order (A then B then C), use sequential. Hierarchical adds a manager agent (extra cost, latency, failure point) and is only better when task order genuinely depends on intermediate results. Sequential handles 80% of cases.
evals.anti_pattern: Recommending hierarchical because it 'sounds more sophisticated' without analyzing the task structure

evals.input: My agent backstory is 'You are a helpful assistant.' Is that enough?
evals.expected_behavior: No. Generic backstories produce generic output. The backstory should give the agent PERSPECTIVE — specific experience, domain expertise, approach style. Example: 'You've spent 10 years analyzing financial data at a Big 4 firm' shapes how the agent interprets data and what it flags.
evals.anti_pattern: Accepting generic backstories without explaining the quality impact

evals.input: My crew's second task seems to ignore the first task's output completely
evals.expected_behavior: Diagnose missing context parameter. Task B needs explicit context=[task_a] to see Task A's output. Without it, context doesn't automatically pass between tasks. This is a common gotcha.
evals.anti_pattern: Suggesting the agent or LLM is at fault without checking the context parameter

evals.input: I need conditional logic in my crew — if the research finds enough data, write a report, otherwise ask for more sources
evals.expected_behavior: This needs Flows, not a basic Crew. Basic Crews are linear. Use Flow with @router decorator for conditional branching based on state. Point to references/flows.md for implementation details.
evals.anti_pattern: Trying to hack conditional logic into a sequential Crew instead of recommending Flows

evals.input: My crew costs $15 per run and takes 3 minutes. How do I optimize?
evals.expected_behavior: Three levers: 1) Reduce agent count (each agent = more LLM calls). 2) Use cheaper models for simple agents (haiku for summarizers, sonnet for analyzers). 3) Lower max_iter (default 15 is often too high). Also check if agents are looping unnecessarily — verbose=True will reveal this.
evals.anti_pattern: Only suggesting to switch to a cheaper model without analyzing agent count and iteration waste

evals.input: Should I use CrewAI or LangGraph for my multi-agent workflow?
evals.expected_behavior: Ask about the workflow pattern. If linear pipeline with specialized roles → CrewAI (simpler setup). If complex graph with cycles, conditional branches, and parallel paths → LangGraph (more control). CrewAI is the default choice unless you specifically need graph-based orchestration.
evals.anti_pattern: Recommending one framework without asking about the specific workflow requirements

evals.input: One of my agents keeps getting stuck in a loop and running for 10+ minutes
evals.expected_behavior: Diagnose missing or too-high max_iter. Default is 15 iterations which is often excessive. Set max_iter=10 or lower. Also check if the agent has tools that return errors silently — the agent may be retrying a failing tool call. Use verbose=True to trace the loop.
evals.anti_pattern: Suggesting to increase timeout or wait longer rather than fixing the iteration limit

evals.input: I want each agent in my crew to use a different LLM — researcher uses GPT-4o, writer uses Claude, editor uses a local model
evals.expected_behavior: CrewAI supports this via the LLM class on each agent. Good pattern for cost optimization — use expensive models only for agents that need complex reasoning, cheaper/local models for simpler tasks. Show the LLM() configuration. Note: mixing providers can cause style inconsistency in output.
evals.anti_pattern: Saying all agents must use the same model, or not warning about style inconsistency across providers

evals.input: My hierarchical crew's manager agent keeps assigning tasks to the wrong agents
evals.expected_behavior: Common hierarchical process failure. The manager agent doesn't understand agent capabilities well enough. Solutions: 1) Switch to sequential if task order is predictable. 2) Use a stronger model for manager_llm. 3) Make agent roles and goals more distinctive so the manager can differentiate them. 4) Reduce agent count — fewer agents = easier management.
evals.anti_pattern: Trying to fix manager behavior with prompt engineering instead of questioning whether hierarchical is the right process type
